text.title: Service Blueprint Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules that distinguish a service blueprint from a glossy diagram. A blueprint that fails any rule is a journey map in disguise.

rule.statement: The blueprint MUST contain exactly five swimlanes in this order: (1) Customer Actions, (2) Frontstage Interactions, (3) Line of Visibility, (4) Backstage Actions, (5) Support Processes & Systems. The "Line of Visibility" is a horizontal divider, not a free row; what is BELOW it is invisible to the customer. Missing or merged lanes = REJECT.
rule.rationale: The line of visibility is the entire point of the blueprint — it isolates "what the user sees" from "what causes the user's experience." Without it, the artefact regresses to a journey map.

rule.statement: The Support Processes & Systems lane MUST name specific internal systems (e.g. Salesforce, Zendesk, Stripe Billing, Snowflake) per journey step — not generic labels ("CRM", "billing"). Unnamed systems = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Generic labels don't help engineering or ops. Naming the real system surfaces "we need a Zendesk macro" vs "we need a Salesforce integration" — different teams, different work.

rule.statement: Every observed friction point on the journey lane MUST trace a "failure arrow" downward through the Line of Visibility to the specific backstage step or system that caused it. Friction points without a downward causal trace = REJECT — that's the gap this methodology exists to close.
rule.rationale: "User got frustrated at billing" is useless to ops; "user got frustrated because the Stripe dunning email sends 7 days before the support team gets notified" is fixable. The arrow IS the fix-target.

rule.statement: The blueprint MUST tag ≥ 3 "moments of truth" (high-impact steps that determine satisfaction/churn) with: impact score (1-5), evidence source (interviews / NPS / support tickets / churn analysis), and a hypothesised root cause. Unscored MoTs = REJECT.
rule.rationale: A flat blueprint suggests all steps matter equally; in reality 3-5 moments dominate. Scoring forces prioritization, which is the only way the blueprint produces a backlog.

rule.statement: Every backstage action MUST name the role/team that performs it (Support Agent / Onboarding Specialist / Fulfilment Op / Finance Ops / SRE). "The team does X" = REJECT. Roles can be plural (e.g. "Tier-1 Support") but cannot be company-wide ("we").
rule.rationale: Vague backstage actors hide work; once the role is named you can ask whether that role has the tools/training/SLA to do it well. RACI inputs to design changes follow directly.

example.title: Worked friction trace
example.text: step: "user contacts support about billing discrepancy" customer_action: opens chat widget frontstage: support agent receives ticket in Zendesk (T+0) line_of_visibility: ───────── backstage_action: agent attempts to verify invoice (T+2min) support_system: Zendesk macro queries Salesforce; Salesforce DOES NOT carry Stripe invoice line-items → agent must open Stripe dashboard (T+5min) MoT_score: 5/5 (interview evidence: 6/10 churned users cite this step) root_cause: Stripe→Salesforce data sync missing; design backlog item: SAL-INVOICE-LINES-IMPORT
